narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—higher than the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 30. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on past students' academic records, family details, and demographics, flagged concerns in your early performance. Specifically, it calculated an 88.2% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, leading directly to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the average of around the 5th or 6th decile—and signaled to the model a significant drop-off in performance during that key later stage. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 2, also well below the typical 5th or 6th decile average, reinforced this pattern of lower early rankings, nudging the prediction further toward failure. Positively, your above-average undergraduate GPA worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction back toward a higher chance of passing and showing the model your potential outside of school rankings. Compared to typical students in the dataset, where most are full-time like you, your academic deciles were notably weaker, outweighing strengths like your GPA and overriding what might otherwise be a more balanced profile for someone from your background. This combination made the model view your bar exam risk as too high for admission. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the 36.7 average, as that could tip future predictions in your favor. Strengthening recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate improvement. Reach out to admissions advisors at other schools for holistic reviews, and perhaps connect with mentors from similar backgrounds who've navigated bar success. With targeted steps, you can reshape how models—and committees—see your potential. Keep pushing; your story isn't over.
